public String reverseParentheses(String s) {
    Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
    List<int[]> pairs = new ArrayList<>();

    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
        char c = s.charAt(i);
        if (c == '(') {
            stack.push(i);
        } else if (c == ')') {
            if (!stack.isEmpty()) {
                int start = stack.pop();
                int level = stack.size() + 1; // because after popping, the size is one less than before.
                pairs.add(new int[]{start, i, level});
            }
        }
    }

    // Sort pairs in descending order of level
    pairs.sort((a, b) -> Integer.compare(b[2], a[2]));

    char[] arr = s.toCharArray();

    for (int[] pair : pairs) {
        int start = pair[0];
        int end = pair[1];
        // Reverse substring from start+1 to end-1
        int left = start + 1;
        int right = end - 1;
        while (left < right) {
            char temp = arr[left];
            arr[left] = arr[right];
            arr[right] = temp;
            left++;
            right--;
        }
    }

    return new String(arr);
}

Wait, but in the code above, the level is calculated as stack.size() + 1 after popping. Is that correct?

Wait, no. Because when we pop the start index, the stack size decreases by 1. So the level is the size before popping.

So, the correct code is:

int level = stack.size();

then, start = stack.pop();

    if (!stack.isEmpty()) {
        int level = stack.size();
        int start = stack.pop();
        pairs.add(new int[]{start, i, level});
    }
}

solution: 
```java
import java.util.ArrayDeque;
import java.util.Deque;
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

class Solution {
    public String reverseParentheses(String s) {
        Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        List<int[]> pairs = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            char c = s.charAt(i);
            if (c == '(') {
                stack.push(i);
            } else if (c == ')') {
                if (!stack.isEmpty()) {
                    int level = stack.size();
                    int start = stack.pop();
                    pairs.add(new int[]{start, i, level});
                }
            }
        }

        pairs.sort((a, b) -> Integer.compare(b[2], a[2]));

        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();

        for (int[] pair : pairs) {
            int start = pair[0];
            int end = pair[1];
            int left = start + 1;
            int right = end - 1;
            while (left < right) {
                char temp = arr[left];
                arr[left] = arr[right];
                arr[right] = temp;
                left++;
                right--;
            }
        }

        return new String(arr);
    }
}
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O(n^2)

space complexity: O(n)

energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution efficiently handles nested parentheses by collecting all matching pairs, sorting them by nesting level, and reversing the substrings in the correct order. This approach ensures that each reversal is done in the correct order, starting from the innermost parentheses, resulting in the desired output. The use of a stack to track parentheses and a list to store pairs allows for efficient processing. The overall complexity is O(n^2) due to the nested reversal operations, which is efficient given the problem constraints.
